Class TestClickCombination

java.lang.Object
java.lang.Thread
com.github.mrgarbagegamer.TestClickCombination
All Implemented Interfaces:
Runnable
public class TestClickCombination extends Thread
An optimized worker thread that tests potential puzzle solutions from a shared work queue.

This class implements the "consumer" role in the solver's producer-consumer architecture. Each instance, referred to as a "monkey," runs on a dedicated thread. Its primary responsibility is to fetch WorkBatch objects from the CombinationQueueArray, test the thousands of combinations within each batch against a local Grid clone, and validate them.

The name "monkey" is a playful nod to the "infinite monkey theorem." In our context, the monkeys are tirelessly testing combinations on the grid, hoping to find the one that solves the puzzle. This terminology is used throughout the codebase to add a bit of whimsy and distinguish these worker threads from the "generator" threads.

Execution Model

Monkeys operate in a simple, continuous loop. They do not generate their own work; instead, they pull batches from a queue system. Each monkey has a preferred queue but will steal work from other queues if its own is empty.

For each combination, the monkey performs a highly-optimized odd adjacency check. This acts as a final, more stringent filter than the pruning done by generators, significantly reducing the number of expensive grid state manipulations. If a combination passes this check, it is applied to the grid. If it solves the puzzle, the monkey logs the solution, signals a global shutdown, and terminates.

Resource Management

To eliminate contention, each monkey operates on its own private Grid instance. Unlike generator tasks, which use ThreadLocal storage to manage resources within a ForkJoinPool, monkeys are simple Threads with a persistent state, avoiding the overhead of ThreadLocal lookups in their hot loop.

The only shared, mutable resource monkeys interact with directly is the central CombinationQueueArray, from which they fetch work and to which they recycle WorkBatch objects after use. This design ensures that no objects are allocated in the hot path, with the minor exception of logging.

Performance and Critical Paths

The performance of the entire solver is heavily influenced by the efficiency of the monkey's main run loop and, most importantly, the satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[]) check. These sections are heavily optimized to be JIT-friendly, minimizing branching and using bitwise operations for fast validation.

Since:
2025.04 - Multi-threaded Solver Introduction
Performance Characteristics:
The main loop's overall time complexity is O(CombinationGeneratorTask.BATCH_SIZE) per batch, dominated by the O(combination.length) odd adjacency check performed for each combination.
Threading Model:
Each monkey is a Thread that operates on its own Grid instance. Work is obtained in a thread-safe manner from the shared CombinationQueueArray.
Algorithm Details:
Pulls a WorkBatch (from its own queue or by stealing), then iterates through its combinations. Each is validated with an odd adjacency check. Valid combinations are tested on the grid. On success, the monkey triggers a global shutdown.
Memory Management:
Fixed memory footprint with minimal allocations, except for logging.

  • Field Details

    • logger

      private static final Logger logger
      The logger for this class.

      Logging is used to report significant events, such as finding a solution, interruptions, and periodic progress updates. To minimize performance impact on the worker threads, an asynchronous Log4j2 logger is used, which offloads I/O operations to a separate background thread.

      Since:
      2025.05 - Async Logging Introduction
      See Also:
      • run()
      • CombinationMessage
      • Logger.debug(String)
      • Logger.info(String, Object)
      • LogManager.getLogger()
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) for logger retrieval.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe per Log4j2 design.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes for the static reference.

    • LOG_EVERY_N_FAILURES

      private static final int LOG_EVERY_N_FAILURES
      A constant defining the frequency of logging for failed attempts.

      To avoid overwhelming the logs and impacting performance, a debug entry for a failed combination is made only once per this many failures. This check applies only to combinations that have already passed the satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[]) check.

      Since:
      2025.05 - Logging Threshold Introduction
      See Also:
      • run()
      • CombinationMessage
      • Constant Field Values
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) retrieval for checks.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe as a static final constant.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

    • combinationQueue

      private final CombinationQueue combinationQueue
      The monkey's preferred CombinationQueue.

      Each monkey has an assigned queue to pull work from. This affinity helps reduce contention. If this queue is empty, the monkey will attempt to steal work from other queues in the shared queueArray.

      Since:
      2025.05 - Dedicated Queue per Monkey
      See Also:
      • TestClickCombination(String, CombinationQueue, CombinationQueueArray, Grid)
      • allQueuesEmpty()
      • getWork()
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) dequeue operations.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe through JCTools wizardry.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes for the reference.

    • queueArray

      private final CombinationQueueArray queueArray
      A reference to the shared CombinationQueueArray.

      This provides access to all work queues for stealing, the shared WorkBatch pool for recycling, and global state flags like CombinationQueueArray.solutionFound and CombinationQueueArray.generationComplete.

      Since:
      2025.05 - Dedicated Queue per Monkey
      See Also:
      • TestClickCombination(String, CombinationQueue, CombinationQueueArray, Grid)
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access to queues and flags.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe through use of volatile flags and concurrent queues.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes for the reference.

    • puzzleGrid

      private final Grid puzzleGrid
      The monkey's dedicated Grid instance for testing combinations.

      To prevent any thread contention, each monkey operates on a private clone of the puzzle grid. The grid's state is reset after each failed combination test.

      Since:
      2025.04 - Multi-threaded Solver Introduction
      See Also:
      • run()
      • TestClickCombination(String, CombinationQueue, CombinationQueueArray, Grid)
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) state resets and cell toggles.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe by design, as each thread has its own instance.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes for the reference.

    • CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK

      private static volatile long[] CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK
      A pre-computed lookup table mapping a cell click to its effect on the puzzle's true cells.

      This table is the core of the satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[]) check. Each index corresponds to a cell on the grid (0-108). The long value at that index is a bitmask where the Nth bit is 1 if clicking that cell toggles the Nth true cell of the puzzle.

      This table is initialized once per puzzle via initializeLookupTable(short[]) using double-checked locking for thread-safety. All monkey threads share this single static instance. Since all puzzles have fewer than 64 true cells, a single long is sufficient for the bitmask.

      Since:
      2025.06 - Bitmask Pre-computations
      See Also:
      • EXPECTED_MASK
      • satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[])
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(NUM_CELLS) initialization cost amortized across threads; O(1) lookups during checks.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe via double-checked locking during initialization.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of ~8 × Grid.NUM_CELLS bytes (872 bytes).

    • EXPECTED_MASK

      private static volatile long EXPECTED_MASK
      The target bitmask for a valid combination, where all bits corresponding to true cells are 1.

      A combination is valid under the odd adjacency rule if the final result of XORing the CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK for each click equals this expected mask. This means every true cell was toggled an odd number of times.

      Like the lookup table, this value is computed once per puzzle via initializeLookupTable(short[]) and shared statically.

      Since:
      2025.06 - Bitmask Pre-computations
      See Also:
      • CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK
      • satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[])
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) lookups.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe via double-checked locking during initialization.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 8 bytes as a primitive long.

  • Constructor Details

    • TestClickCombination

      public TestClickCombination(String threadName, CombinationQueue combinationQueue, CombinationQueueArray queueArray, Grid puzzleGrid)
      Constructs a monkey thread.

      Each monkey requires its own Grid instance to test combinations, a preferred CombinationQueue to pull work from, and a reference to the shared CombinationQueueArray for work-stealing and global state coordination.

      This constructor also triggers the one-time, thread-safe initialization of the static CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK and EXPECTED_MASK lookup tables, which are shared across all monkey instances for a given puzzle.

      Performance Considerations

      The design delegates resource allocation to the caller for flexibility. A potential micro-optimization could be to make the queueArray a static field, set once, rather than passing it as a reference to each monkey. This would save a small amount of memory per thread but was omitted for design simplicity.

      Parameters:
      threadName - The unique name for this monkey thread, used for logging.
      combinationQueue - The monkey's preferred work queue.
      queueArray - The shared array of all queues, used for work-stealing and coordination.
      puzzleGrid - A unique Grid instance for this monkey to use for testing.
      Since:
      2025.04 - Monkey Thread Introduction
      See Also:
      • combinationQueue
      • puzzleGrid
      • queueArray
      • Grid.findTrueCells(Grid.ValueFormat)
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) assignments. The one-time cost of initializeLookupTable(short[]) is amortized across all threads.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe by nature of construction.
      Memory Management:
      Allocates only a temporary short[] during the first lookup table initialization, in addition to the thread itself.

  • Method Details

    • initializeLookupTable

      private static void initializeLookupTable(short[] trueCells)
      Initializes the static, shared lookup tables for the odd adjacency check.

      This method pre-computes the CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK and EXPECTED_MASK values. It is called from the constructor but uses double-checked locking to ensure it only executes once per puzzle, making initialization thread-safe and lazy.

      For each of the 109 possible clicks, it determines which of the puzzle's true cells are adjacent and encodes that information into a bitmask. It then computes the final expected mask where all relevant bits are set.

      Parameters:
      trueCells - The array of initially true cells in Index format.
      Since:
      2025.07 - Long Array Grid State Representation
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(Grid.NUM_CELLS × trueCells.length) initialization.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe due to double-checked locking.
      Memory Management:
      Allocates the lookup table (approx. 872 bytes) once.

    • run

      public void run()
      The main execution loop for the monkey thread.

      This loop continuously fetches and processes WorkBatch objects until a solution is found or all work is complete.

      Algorithm

      1. Attempt to get a work batch.
      2. If no work is found, check for termination conditions (solution found, or generation is complete and all queues are empty). If no reason to terminate, sleep briefly and continue.
      3. If a batch is acquired, iterate through each combination within it.
      4. For each combination, perform the satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[]) check.
      5. If the check passes, apply the clicks to the local puzzleGrid.
      6. If the grid is solved, log the solution, trigger a global shutdown, and terminate.
      7. If the grid is not solved, reset it and continue.
      8. After the batch is exhausted, recycle it to the shared pool and repeat the loop.

      Performance & Future Optimizations

      The loop is structured to be JIT-friendly. A key future optimization would be a more direct cancellation mechanism. Instead of polling the solutionFound flag, a direct interrupt or signal to all worker threads would be more efficient. Additionally, the logging of failed combinations currently requires cloning an array and creating a CombinationMessage, which could be optimized to be allocation-free.

      Specified by:
      run in interface Runnable
      Overrides:
      run in class Thread
      Since:
      2025.04 - Monkey Thread Introduction
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(CombinationGeneratorTask.BATCH_SIZE) per batch, excluding the cost of retrieval, logging, and checks.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; independent state per thread, shared access to concurrent structures.
      Algorithm Details:
      Continuously pulls batches, iterates through combinations, performs checks, tests on the grid, and handles success or recycling.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate, except for logging.

    • triggerGeneratorShutdown

      private void triggerGeneratorShutdown()
      Triggers an immediate shutdown of the generator ForkJoinPool.

      When a solution is found, this method is called to halt the creation of new work. It directly invokes ForkJoinPool.shutdownNow() on the static pool instance held by CombinationGeneratorTask. This is more efficient than relying on cancellation flags, as it uses the ForkJoinPool's built-in, low-overhead mechanism to interrupt all active generator tasks.

      Since:
      2025.07.23 - Explicit Cancellation Checks Removal
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) shutdown signaling.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe as per ForkJoinPool design.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate (except for logging).

    • getWork

      private WorkBatch getWork()
      Obtains a WorkBatch to process, using a work-stealing strategy.

      This method first attempts to poll from the monkey's preferred queue. If that is empty, it iterates through all other queues in the shared CombinationQueueArray to "steal" a batch. This non-blocking approach allows idle monkeys to pick up work from busy threads, helping to balance the load.

      A more advanced implementation might use some load-balanced algorithm for stealing to reduce contention, but the current linear scan is simple and effective enough.

      Returns:
      A WorkBatch to process, or null if no work is available anywhere.
      Since:
      2025.07 - Enqueueing Work Batches
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) to steal from the preferred queue, and O(queues.length) in the worst case for stealing.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; uses non-blocking queue operations.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate.

    • allQueuesEmpty

      private boolean allQueuesEmpty()
      Checks if all work queues in the system are empty.

      This is a key part of the shutdown logic. A monkey can only safely terminate when generation is complete and all queues are empty (or if the solution is found), ensuring no work is left unprocessed. This check is only performed when a monkey is idle, so its O(queues.length) complexity is acceptable.

      Returns:
      true if all queues are empty, false otherwise.
      Since:
      2025.08 - Preallocate WorkBatches for Queues
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(queues.length) in the worst case due to iteration.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; calls a thread-safe method on each queue.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate.

    • satisfiesOddAdjacency

      private final boolean satisfiesOddAdjacency(short[] combination)
      Checks if a combination is valid based on the "odd adjacency" rule.

      This is the most performance-critical method in the monkey's hot loop. It provides a final, highly-efficient check to prune invalid combinations before they are tested on the expensive Grid object.

      The logic relies on a property of this puzzle type: for a solution to be valid, every initially true cell must be toggled an odd number of times. This method validates this by XORing the pre-computed CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK for each click in the combination. If the final result equals the EXPECTED_MASK, the rule is satisfied.

      Performance & JIT Optimizations

      The method is heavily optimized for the JIT compiler. It uses local final variables to cache array references and loop bounds, which encourages the JIT to perform optimizations like loop unrolling. The core logic is a tight loop of XOR operations, which is extremely fast on modern CPUs.

      Further SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) optimizations were considered but were deemed impractical for the following reasons:

      • Vector API: While promising, it suffered from excessive heap allocations for vector operations in tested JDK versions, negating the performance benefits. This may become viable with future improvements like Project Valhalla.
      • JNI (Java Native Interface): The overhead of marshalling data between Java and native code for each combination would outweigh the gains from using C++ intrinsics. Batching calls would introduce significant complexity in managing the return values.
      • JIT Auto-Vectorization: This is the most desirable approach, but the JIT is currently unable to vectorize this loop due to a data dependency (the running XOR) and the indirect array access pattern (masks[click]).
      Manual loop unrolling or restructuring the loop into a gather-reduce pattern remain potential, albeit more complex, future optimizations if this method becomes a bottleneck.

      Parameters:
      combination - The combination of clicks to check, in Index format.
      Returns:
      true if the combination is valid, false otherwise.
      Since:
      2025.06 - Odd Adjacency Pruning for Monkeys
      See Also:
      • Grid.areAdjacent(short, short, Grid.ValueFormat)
      • Grid.findTrueCells(Grid.ValueFormat)
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(combination.length) iteration, dominated by XOR operations.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; uses only static final data and local variables.
      Algorithm Details:
      Iterates through the combination, XORing the pre-computed CLICK_TO_TRUE_CELL_MASK for each click, and compares the final result to the EXPECTED_MASK.
      Memory Management:
      Does not allocate.

Performance-optimized brute-force solver for hexagonal grid Lights Out puzzles
